content.problemStatement:
Chef is teaching his class of NNN students at Hogwarts. He groups students with the same height together for an activity. Some of the students end up in a groups with only themselves and are saddened by this.

With the help of his magic wand, Chef can **increase** the height of any student to any value he likes. Now Chef wonders, what is the minimum number of students whose height needs to be increased so that there are no sad students?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of students.
The second line consists of NNN space-separated integers H1,H2,…,HNH_1, H_2, \ldots, H_NH1​,H2​,…,HN​ denoting the heights of the students.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the minimum number of students whose heights must to be increased.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 3*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of students. The second line contains N space‑separated integers H1, H2, …, HN – the heights of the students.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of students whose height must be increased so that every height appears at least twice.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: each height appears twice, no changes needed.
Test case 2: height 1 appears once; increase it to 2 (one change).
Test case 3: all heights equal, no changes.
Test case 4: five distinct heights → 5 singletons → ceil(5/2)=3 changes.
Heights 2 and 4 appear once (2 singletons). Pair them by increasing 2 to 4 (or 4 to a higher value). Only one student’s height changes.
Both students already share the same height; no changes required.

solution.approach: For each test case count how many distinct heights appear exactly once (singletons). Let s be that count. Two singletons can be merged by increasing the lower height to match the higher one, which costs only one change. Therefore we can pair up the singletons, using one change per pair, and if an odd singleton remains we must change it as well. The minimal number of changes is ceil(s/2) = (s+1)//2. All other heights already appear at least twice and need no modification.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case to count frequencies, overall O(total N) across all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(K) where K is the number of distinct heights in a test case (at most N).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
from collections import Counter

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        heights = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        freq = Counter(heights)
        singletons = sum(1 for v in freq.values() if v == 1)
        out_lines.append(str((singletons + 1) // 2))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
